stem: For which value of \( k \) is the matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} k & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[g]] \end{bmatrix} \) singular (that is, \( \det(A) = 0 \))?
solution: Expanding along the last row, \( \det(A) = [[g]]\,(k\cdot[[e]] - ([[b]])([[d]])) \). Since \( [[g]] \ne 0 \), this is zero when \( [[e]]k = ([[b]])([[d]]) \), giving \( k = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the product \( bd \); one must still divide by \( [[e]] \) to solve \( [[e]]k = bd \).
This solves the determinant equation with the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: For which value of \( k \) is the matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & k \end{bmatrix} \) singular?
solution: The matrix is singular exactly when \( \det(A) = [[a]]k - ([[b]])([[c]]) = 0 \), i.e. \( k = \dfrac{([[b]])([[c]])}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the product \( bc \); one must still divide by \( a \) to isolate \( k \).
This solves the determinant equation with the wrong sign.
This is the top-right entry, not the value of \( k \).
This is the bottom-left entry, not the value of \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: For which value of \( k \) does \( A = \begin{bmatrix} k & [[b]] & 0 \\ [[c]] & k & 0 \\ [[e]] & [[f]] & [[g]] \end{bmatrix} \) satisfy \( \det(A) = 0 \)? (There are two such values; give a value of \( k \) that works.)
solution: Expanding along the third column, \( \det(A) = [[g]]\,(k^2 - [[b]]\cdot[[c]]) \). Since \( [[b]]\cdot[[c]] = ([[m]])^2\cdot[[b]]^2 \), this is zero when \( k = \pm [[b]]\cdot[[m]] \); in particular \( k = [[answer]] \) works.
distractor_rationales: This is \( m \) alone, before multiplying by the top-middle entry \( b \).
This has the right form but the wrong sign for one of the two roots (or the other root's magnitude).
This is off by one from a correct root.
This is off by one from a correct root.
This is off by two from a correct root.
This has the wrong sign and is off by one.
This is the bottom-right entry \( g \), not a root of the determinant equation.

stem: The third row of \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[c]] \\ [[ma]] & [[mb]] & k \end{bmatrix} \) is exactly [[m]] times the first row in its first two entries. For which value of \( k \) is \( A \) singular (i.e. the third row becomes exactly [[m]] times the first row)?
solution: Row 3 is already \([[m]]\cdot([[a]]), [[m]]\cdot([[b]])\) in its first two entries, matching \([[m]]\) times row 1. It becomes exactly \([[m]]\) times row 1 (making \(A\) singular, since a row is a scalar multiple of another) precisely when its last entry also matches: \( k = [[m]]\cdot([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the first row's last entry itself, without scaling by [[m]].
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is the (already-scaled) first entry of row 3, not the value needed for the last entry.
This is the (already-scaled) second entry of row 3, not the value needed for the last entry.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
